9 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Image generated using ClaudeDesign
Why LangGraph?
Below is an analogy that will help understanding the core intent of LangGraph existence!
Analogy Explained: Imagine an old paper map versus a modern GPS. A paper map shows you one fixed route. If a road is closed, the map can’t react. That’s the straight-line route and LangChain basics.
A GPS is alive. It knows where you are right now. It reroutes when you hit traffic. It remembers your destination even when you take a wrong turn. It can pause and ask, “There’s a toll ahead — do you want to take it?” And if your phone dies, it picks up exactly where you left off when it comes back on — this is LangGraph for you.
LangGraph is the GPS for your AI application. It lets you describe your app as a set of steps connected by arrows — where the arrows can loop, split, and wait — and it keeps a live, shared memory of what’s happening the whole way through.
That’s it. Everything technical below is just that idea, spelled out. Let me provide more context with below examples and theories:
If you have built anything with llms lately, you have almost certainly used LangChain. It is a great place to start but…The trouble shows up when we try to ship. Real products need to loop, pause for a person, survive a crash, and sometimes hand work between several AIs.
That is the exact set of problems LangGraph was built for. Here is the idea in one line:
Theoretically: LangChain models your app as a straight line. LangGraph models it as a map with arrows that can loop back, branch, and pause.
In Layman Terms: LangChain modelled an LLM app like a recipe: do step 1, then 2, then 3, then serve. LangGraph models it like a whiteboard with arrows — you can loop back, branch on conditions, pause for a human, pick up where you left off.
Why LangGraph was actually created: LangChain’s original abstractions — LLMChain, SequentialChain, and later LCEL's prompt | llm | parser — gave the world a clean way to express linear LLM pipelines. The model is elegant when work flows in one direction. It breaks when work needs to loop back, branch based on intermediate results, pause for human approval, or coordinate multiple agents.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Image generated using ClaudeDesignKeyConcept: LangGraph is not “LangChain plus loops.” It is a different abstraction: control flow becomes a graph, state becomes the shared substrate, and persistence + HITL are baked into the runtime. The chain is a special case of the graph (one node, two edges).
(1) Chains are linear DAGs; agentic systems need cycles, branching, and shared state — the graph abstraction expresses these directly.
(2) Persistence and interrupts let you build long-running, human-supervised workflows without custom infrastructure.
(3) LangGraph is built on LangChain’s Runnable interface, so you keep streaming, tracing, batching — you are not choosing one over the other, you are composing them.
Below are the few parameters where both LangChain and LangGraph can be compared easily for fundamentals understanding.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

The mental shift. Chains answer “what happens next?” with a fixed sequence. Graphs answer it with a function of state. That is the conceptual jump: control flow becomes data-dependent, and the application becomes inspectable as a topology rather than executed as a script.

Core Components
Graph: A map of steps joined by arrows. Unlike a straight checklist, the arrows can loop back to an earlier step or branch in different directions depending on what happened.
Node: A single unit of work — just a Python function. It reads the current situation and does one thing: call the model, run a tool, check a result.
Edge: The connection between two nodes that says “go here next.” A conditional edge chooses the next step by looking at the current situation.
State: One shared notebook every step reads from and writes to. Instead of passing a note hand-to-hand, all steps see and update the same page.
Agent: An LLM that can decide and act in a loop: think about the goal, use a tool, look at the result, then decide the next move — until the job is done.
CheckPoint: An automatic save of the notebook after each round of work — so the app can pause, wait for a human, crash and recover, or resume hours later from the same spot.
LangGraph is not the first graph-based orchestrator. It sits inside a lineage that includes finite-state machines, BPMN engines, Apache Airflow DAGs, Temporal workflows, and Google’s Pregel system. Each of those tools solves a slightly different problem, and understanding the differences sharpens the sense of when LangGraph is the right choice.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Image generated using ClaudeDesign

API surface
The fundamentals from previous Section are the conceptual primitives. This section is the API surface — the actual classes, functions, and constants you’ll import. We'll cover what each one is for in brief with the help of below table.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Image generated using ClaudeDesign

Execution Model
Let us know what happens under the hood while debugging or designing retries.
Super-steps — the Pregel heartbeat
Think of the graph running in rounds, like turns in a board game. In each round, every step that is “ready to go” plays at the same time. When they all finish, their results are merged into the shared notebook, and that decides who is ready to go next round. One such round is a super-step. The whole run is just rounds repeating until nobody is ready to move. That is the entire execution model — everything below is the precise version of this one picture.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Image generated using ClaudeDesign
LangGraph borrows the bulk-synchronous-parallel (BSP) execution model from Google’s Pregel. A run proceeds in discrete super-steps. In each super-step:

• The runtime identifies all active nodes — nodes that have received a message (state update) on an incoming edge.

• All active nodes execute in parallel (subject to a configurable concurrency limit).

• Each node returns a state update. The runtime applies reducers to merge updates into the shared state.

• The new state is checkpointed (if a checkpointer is configured).

• Edges from completed nodes activate their downstream nodes. The next super-step begins.

• When no nodes are active and no messages are in transit, execution halts
Why this matters in practice. Super-steps make execution deterministic and inspectable. Every checkpoint is between super-steps, never inside one. Parallel branches that write to the same key go through the reducer — race-free. The framework’s debugging output, stream_mode="updates", emits one event per super-step. If you understand super-steps, every other debugging tool clicks into place.
State transitions >> event-driven activation >> Graph Traversal
Nodes do not poll. They are activated by incoming messages — i.e., by edges that fire because their source node completed. This is event-driven, not procedural. A node with no incoming activations in a super-step simply does not run. A node with two incoming activations in the same super-step runs once, with the merged state. The runtime tracks which nodes are “live” and stops when none are.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Graph traversal — branching, joining, looping
Recursion limits and infinite-loop protection
Every run has a recursion limit — the maximum number of super-steps before the runtime aborts with a GraphRecursionError. The default is 25.
When you hit the recursion limit: it’s almost always one of three things — a router that always picks “continue”, a tool-call loop where the model never produces a final answer, or a forgotten edge to END. Trace the last few super-steps in LangSmith; the loop will be obvious.

Building AI Agents with LangGraph
“Agent” is an overloaded word. In LangGraph, an agent is a graph whose control flow is driven (at least in part) by an LLM’s decisions — choose a tool, choose to continue, choose to revise. The patterns below are the recurring shapes. They are not mutually exclusive; production systems often combine several.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Image generated using ClaudeDesign
Multi-Agent Systems
A single agent eventually hits one of three walls. Context exhaustion: too many tools, too long a prompt, too much accumulated history — the model gets confused. Specialisation conflict: a “do-everything” agent prompt-engineered to be a great researcher is mediocre at coding, and vice versa. Coordination needs: tasks require multiple parallel actors with different roles. The answer is to split into multiple agents that talk to each other through structured handoffs.
Why multi-agent?

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Image generated using ClaudeDesign
The five canonical multi-agent patterns
LangGraph’s multi-agent vocabulary clusters into five patterns. They differ along two axes: who decides what runs next (centralised supervisor vs decentralised handoff) and how state flows (shared message history vs isolated per-agent state).

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Image generated using ClaudeDesign
Choosing the right pattern — decision guide

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Image generated using ClaudeDesign
Multi-agent is a structural answer to specialisation, context, and coordination problems — not a default architecture. The supervisor pattern is the sensible starting point for most teams; router is the fastest; hierarchical is for when the team grows up; swarm is for conversational systems. The pattern you pick is a function of where the coordination decision lives — and that’s the question to ask first.
When does single agent beat multi-agent system?
If the task is small and focused, one agent is faster, cheaper, and easier to debug. Multiple agents only pay off when the work truly splits into different specialities.
Default to single-agent. Multi-agent earns its place when one of three thresholds is crossed:
(1) the tool count is large enough that the model’s tool-selection accuracy degrades (typically > 10 tools in one prompt);
(2) the specialities are genuinely separable — a “be a great researcher and a great coder and a great mathematician” prompt is worse than three focused agents;
(3) parallel sub-tasks benefit from per-agent context resets. Below those thresholds, single agents are cheaper, faster, and easier to debug. Frontier-model context windows keep raising the bar — what required multi-agent in 2023 fits in a single agent now.

Further Reading and References

• LangGraph Documentation — Overview · LangChainThe canonical entry point. Start here for the conceptual framing and the “why LangGraph” pitch in the team’s own words.

• LangGraph — Announcement Post · LangChain BlogThe original announcement. Explains the design rationale and the move beyond AgentExecutor's opaque control loop.

• Graph API Overview · LangChain DocsWhere the Pregel super-step model is explained explicitly. Worth reading once, then re-reading when you start debugging parallel execution.

• LangGraph 0.3: Prebuilt Agents · LangChain BlogMarks the split of create_react_agent into langgraph-prebuilt and the broader ecosystem strategy. Important context for the deprecation in favour of create_agent.

• Pregel: A System for Large-Scale Graph Processing · Google ResearchThe intellectual ancestor. Read the abstract and the super-step section — LangGraph’s execution model lifts directly from this paper.

• Multi-agent systems concepts · LangGraph DocsThe canonical taxonomy of supervisor / router / hierarchical / swarm. Read first.

• Multi-agent supervisor tutorial · LangGraph DocsEnd-to-end supervisor with handoff tools. The current canonical implementation pattern.
